description: Welcome to the Altai Republic, a UNESCO World Heritage region of snow-capped peaks, turquoise alpine lakes, and ancient shamanic culture. Called “Russian Tibet” and “Siberian Switzerland,” Altai sits where Russia, Kazakhstan, China, and Mongolia meet. No industry, no pollution – just 2,000 km of pristine mountains, nomadic traditions, and some of Russia’s most powerful landscapes.

Welcome to Kamchatka, a UNESCO World Heritage peninsula where 300 volcanoes meet the Pacific Ocean. This is Russia’s ultimate wilderness – active volcanoes, geysers, hot springs, brown bears fishing for salmon, and zero roads connecting it to mainland Russia. For adventure seekers, Kamchatka is the final frontier. Fly in, helicopter out.

Welcome to Karelia, a pristine region of 60,000 lakes, endless pine forests, and ancient wooden architecture on the border with Finland. Located between St. Petersburg and the Arctic Circle, Karelia is Russia’s answer to Scandinavia – but with Russian soul, UNESCO sites, and unique Karelian culture. Perfect for nature lovers, culture seekers, and FITs wanting “unspoiled Europe”.

Discover Kaliningrad, Russia’s westernmost region and a unique blend of Russian, German, and Baltic cultures. Formerly the Prussian city of Königsberg, this exclave on the Baltic Sea offers medieval castles, amber beaches, and European architecture – all with a Russian visa. No other place in Russia feels like this.

Welcome to Lake Baikal, the world’s deepest, oldest, and clearest freshwater lake. A UNESCO World Heritage Site located in Southern Siberia, Baikal holds 20% of the planet’s unfrozen freshwater. For travelers, it’s not just a lake – it’s a natural wonder offering raw Siberian wilderness, unique culture, and year-round adventure.
Discover Nizhny Novgorod, Russia’s 5th largest city and one of its most underrated gems.
Russia's cultural heart dazzles with baroque palaces, the world-renowned Hermitage Museum, romantic canals, and the legendary White Nights.
Known as the 'Russian Riviera', Sochi blends golden Black Sea beaches with alpine mountain resorts, the lush Sochi National Park, and year-round outdoor adventures.
The multicultural capital of Tatarstan — UNESCO Kazan Kremlin, the grand Qolsharif Mosque and a unique blend of Russian and Eastern cultures.
A circle of ancient Russian towns — Sergiev Posad, Vladimir, Suzdal and Yaroslavl — preserving medieval kremlin walls and Orthodox heritage.
Russia's dynamic capital blends Soviet grandeur with modern energy — from the iconic Red Square and the Kremlin to world-class restaurants, galleries, and a thriving arts scene.
Above the Arctic Circle, Murmansk delivers breathtaking Northern Lights spectacles, polar day adventures, dramatic tundra landscapes, and authentic Russian Arctic culture.
